title: Am I Going Crazy? The Question Every Kundalini Crisis Survivor Asks
meta_description: You're here. It's probably late, maybe really late. Your heart's pounding, your mind's racing, and you're staring at the ceiling, or maybe at this screen,
body:
Am I Going Crazy? The Question Every Kundalini Crisis Survivor Asks

You're here. It's probably late, maybe really late. Your heart's pounding, your mind's racing, and you're staring at the ceiling, or maybe at this screen, wondering if you've finally lost it. That gut-wrenching, bone-deep fear that you're unraveling, that you're slipping into some irreversible madness - yeah, I know that feeling. I've been there. I've lived through those nights where every nerve ending felt like it was on fire, every thought a conspiracy, every sensation a precursor to total collapse. You're not alone. Not by a long shot.

That question, "Am I going crazy?" - it's the mantra of the kundalini crisis. It echoes in your head when your body does things it shouldn't, when your mind shows you things it can't explain, when reality itself feels like a cheap stage prop about to fall over. I'm not here to sugarcoat it. What you're experiencing is intense, it's terrifying, and it feels completely isolating. But here's the thing: it's also a process. A brutal, messy, often misunderstood process. And you're not crazy. You're just going through something that most people don't even have a name for, let alone a roadmap.

I remember one night, I was convinced I was going to spontaneously combust. My body felt like it was vibrating so hard it would just explode into a million pieces. My vision was swimming, sounds were distorted, and I couldn't tell if I was awake or dreaming. I truly believed I was dying, or worse, losing my mind forever. And there was nobody to tell. Nobody who'd understand. My friends would just look at me like I'd sprouted a second head. My family would suggest I get more sleep, or maybe see a doctor who’d prescribe me something that would just numb me out. So I sat there, alone, clutching my head, just trying to breathe. You know that feeling, right? That complete and utter aloneness in the face of something so overwhelming? Yeah, I do. And I'm telling you, what you're feeling, that fear, that disorientation - it's a completely normal reaction to an abnormal experience.

The Crushing Weight of the Unexplained

One of the biggest reasons you feel like you're losing your mind is the sheer lack of explanation. You go to doctors, maybe even therapists, and they're talking about anxiety, maybe psychosis, maybe some obscure neurological condition. And you're sitting there thinking, "No, that's not it. This is different." They don't have a category for the internal tremors, the spontaneous movements, the visions, the intense heat or cold, the electric shocks, the overwhelming bliss followed by crushing despair. They don't have a name for the feeling that your very being is being rewired. So when the experts can't explain it, your mind jumps to the most terrifying conclusion: you're broken, you're malfunctioning, you're crazy.

I've been there, trying to articulate these sensations to someone who's never felt them. It's like trying to describe a color to a blind person. You just can't. And their blank stares, their well-meaning but ultimately unhelpful advice, just makes you feel even more isolated. It reinforces the idea that you're the only one experiencing this, that there's something at its core wrong with you. But here's the truth: what you're experiencing, while rare, isn't unique. There are others. Many others. They've just been as quiet and as scared as you are.

It's Not Psychosis, It's Process

Let's be really clear about something: what you're going through, while it can mimic symptoms of mental illness, isn't necessarily psychosis. Psychosis is a break from reality, a distortion where you can't tell what's real and what's not. With kundalini, you're usually acutely aware that something unusual is happening to you. You might see things, hear things, feel things that aren't "there" in the conventional sense, but you're probably still aware that your perception is altered, that your experience differs from what others perceive. You're questioning your sanity precisely because you're still connected enough to reality to notice the disconnect. A truly psychotic person often doesn't question their own sanity; they believe their altered reality is the real one. Does that land for you? Does it make a difference to hear that?

I spent months convinced I was schizophrenic. I had auditory hallucinations - not voices telling me to do things, but sounds, tones, sometimes what felt like internal conversations that weren't mine. My thoughts would race so fast I couldn't keep up. My body would jerk and twitch uncontrollably. I'd have moments of intense clarity and then moments where I couldn't remember my own name. It was terrifying. But even in the midst of it, there was a part of me, however small, that knew this wasn't what I'd read about in psychiatry textbooks. This felt... different. More energetic, more spiritual, even though I didn't have the language for it then. That tiny spark of awareness, that ability to observe the madness, is what saved me. It's what's saving you too.

You're not losing your mind; your mind is being expanded, stretched, and sometimes, frankly, tortured, by an energy that's much bigger than you. Think of it like this: your consciousness is getting an upgrade, but the hardware - your nervous system, your brain, your body - wasn't ready for the new software. So it glitches. Hard. It's a system overload, not a system failure. You can find more about this in books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). That book was a lifesaver for me, just knowing others had gone through it.

The Body's Rebellion and Rebirth

Then there's the body. Oh, the body. It becomes a battleground, doesn't it? The physical symptoms of kundalini crisis are often the most alarming because they're so tangible, so undeniable, yet so baffling to medical professionals. You might experience:

  • Intense heat or cold, sometimes localized, sometimes全身 (all over your body).
  • Vibrations, tremors, internal shaking that no one else can see.
  • Spontaneous movements, jerks, spasms, sometimes yoga-like postures you've never practiced.
  • Pressure in the head, especially the crown or forehead, sometimes feeling like your skull is expanding.
  • Digestive issues, nausea, loss of appetite, or sudden intense cravings.
  • Heart palpitations, breathlessness, feeling like you can't get enough air.
  • Changes in vision - seeing auras, flashes of light, colors, or distortions.
  • Extreme sensitivity to light, sound, touch, and even other people's emotions.
  • Sexual energy surges, sometimes overwhelming, sometimes painful.
  • Sudden, excruciating pains that move around your body.

I had all of them, at different times, sometimes all at once. My body would shake so hard I couldn't hold a cup of water. My head felt like it was in a vice, and I'd get these electric shocks shooting down my spine. I thought I had a brain tumor, then MS, then some rare nerve disorder. I went through every medical test imaginable, and every single one came back "normal." That's when the real terror set in - if nothing's wrong, then it must be all in my head. Right? That's what they imply. That's what you start to believe.

But here's what I've learned: your body isn't rebelling against you; it's undergoing a radical transformation. This energy, kundalini, it's a powerful force, often described as a serpent coiled at the base of the spine. When it awakens, it moves through your energetic channels, clearing blockages, purifying your system. And that clearing, that purification - it's not a gentle breeze. It's a hurricane. It stirs up everything, all the trauma, all the suppressed emotions, all the physical residues. Your body is screaming because it's being purged, realigned, and upgraded. It's a rebirth, but it feels like death. It's messy. It's painful. But it's also temporary.

Reading The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) helped me understand the physiological side of things. It's clinical, but it validated so much of what I was feeling physically. It showed me that these bizarre symptoms weren't just in my imagination.

The Mind's Labyrinth: Fear, Anxiety, and Despair

Beyond the physical, there's the mental and emotional hellscape. The fear is constant, isn't it? Fear of dying, fear of losing your mind, fear of being stuck like this forever, fear of being alone. It's a primal, existential dread that gnaws at you day and night. And the anxiety - it's not just regular anxiety; it's often a free-floating, all-encompassing panic that comes out of nowhere and grips you tightly. You might feel a deep sense of despair, a hopelessness that makes you question everything, even if life is worth living.

I remember feeling like I was walking a tightrope over an abyss. One wrong step, one bad thought, and I'd plummet into irreversible madness. Every fleeting negative thought felt like a prophecy. Every strange sensation was proof that I was broken. I'd sit there, just trying to breathe, trying to hold onto some semblance of normalcy, but it felt like sand slipping through my fingers. The world seemed darker, colors muted, joy a distant memory. I questioned everything I thought I knew about myself, about reality, about spirituality.

This emotional roller coaster is part of the process too. Kundalini doesn't just clear physical blockages; it brings up all your unresolved emotional trauma, all your shadow aspects, all the stuff you've buried deep down. It forces you to confront it. And that confrontation is terrifying. It's like your subconscious is being emptied out, and everything nasty and painful is bubbling to the surface. It's incredibly disorienting because you're suddenly faced with emotions and memories you thought you'd dealt with, or didn't even know you had. It's like a psychological detox, and just like a physical detox, it feels awful before it feels better.

Finding Your Anchor

So, what do you do when you're in this mind-bending, body-shaking nightmare? You find your anchors. You find anything that can ground you, even if just for a moment. For me, it was simple things: a warm bath, a specific song, the smell of lavender, focusing on my breath. Anything that brought me back to my body, to the present moment, away from the terrifying narratives my mind was spinning. You won't cure it overnight, but you can learn to manage the waves, to ride them instead of drowning in them.

Here are some things that helped me, and might help you:

  • Grounding techniques: Focus on your senses. What do you see, hear, smell, taste, touch right now? Feel your feet on the floor. Hold an ice cube. Splash cold water on your face. These small acts can pull you back from the brink.
  • Routine: Even if it's just getting up at the same time, eating at the same time, trying to sleep at the same time. Routine creates a sense of predictability in a world that feels completely unpredictable.
  • Nature: Get outside. Even if it's just sitting in your yard or by a window. Connect with something real, something natural.
  • Gentle movement: Walking, stretching, gentle yoga. Don't push yourself, but move your body. It helps to discharge some of the intense energy.
  • Creative expression: Don't try to make art; just make. Draw, paint, write, sing, dance. Get it out. It doesn't have to be good; it just has to be.
  • Diet: Eat simple, nourishing foods. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar. Your nervous system is already on overdrive; don't give it more fuel.
  • Connect with others who understand: This is huge. Finding communities, even online, of people who've gone through this can be a lifeline. Just knowing you're not alone is incredibly powerful.

I remember reading The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and feeling a huge wave of relief. It wasn't just my story; it was everyone's story. It had practical advice, not just theoretical explanations. That's what you need right now - practical tools to get through the next hour, the next day.

The Other Side: What Comes Next

You're probably thinking, "Will this ever end?" And the honest answer is, yes, it will. It changes. It cycles. It becomes less intense, more manageable, eventually integrating into who you are. This isn't a permanent state of madness. It's a temporary, albeit brutal, transition. You won't be the same person you were before, but you won't be a broken, crazy person either.

I'm not going to lie - it takes time. Sometimes a long time. Months, even years, to fully integrate. There will be good days and bad days. Days where you feel like you're finally getting somewhere, and days where you feel like you're right back at square one. But each cycle, each wave, is doing its work. It's clearing, it's purifying, it's expanding. You're building resilience you didn't know you had. You're learning about yourself at a depth most people never even glimpse.

When you're in the thick of it, it's hard to see the purpose, hard to see the light at the end of the tunnel. All you see is the darkness, the fear. But trust me, there is a purpose. This process, as terrifying as it is, is ultimately one of deep healing and awakening. It's stripping away everything that isn't truly you, everything that's holding you back from your true potential. It's forcing you to confront your deepest fears and emerge stronger, clearer, and more connected than ever before.

I'll tell you what - when I started to come out of the worst of it, I felt like I'd been through a war. I was exhausted, scarred, but also incredibly alive. My senses were sharper, my intuition stronger, my compassion deeper. I saw the world differently. I saw myself differently. It was like someone had peeled away layers of conditioning and fear, and what was left was... me. The real me. And that's what's waiting for you on the other side of this storm.

If you're still reading this, it means you're still fighting. You're still holding on. And that's a sign of incredible strength. Don't give up. Reach out. Find your people. Read the books like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) that validate your experience. You're not crazy. You're just in the middle of a very intense transformation. Hold on. You'll get through this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
image_alt: Am I Going Crazy? The Question Every Kundalini Crisis Survivor Asks
